project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve the quality and efficiency of water supply services delivered by Participating Urban Water Providers and support urban municipalities to respond to water supply and sanitation emergency needs.
project_overview.total_financing: US$45 million (IDA Credit) + US$1.5 million (municipal counterpart funding) = US$46.5 million total
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 15 Urban Water Providers (UWPs) and up to 360,000 households in urban municipalities with populations over 5,000; priority given to vulnerable groups and low-income consumers

primary_locations: Mid-sized cities and small towns with populations between 5,000-300,000
Specific municipalities to be determined during implementation based on demand-driven approach
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Honduras faces high levels of crime and violence, particularly in urban settings, which is identified as a social risk; however, the project is not specifically targeted at conflict-affected areas but operates in a context of high urban crime and gang violence

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Improving Water Supply Services Provision in Urban Areas
key_components.description: Support for establishment, operationalization, and strengthening of ring-fenced Urban Water Providers (UWPs) through technical assistance, infrastructure rehabilitation, and emergency COVID-19 response. Includes five subcomponents: (1.1) establishment of new UWPs; (1.2) operationalization of developing UWPs; (1.3) strengthening consolidated UWPs; (1.4) enhancing creditworthiness of mixed capital UWPs; (1.5) COVID-19 emergency response including water supply, hygiene activities, and social tariff subsidies.
key_components.financing: US$40 million IDA + US$1.5 million municipal counterpart

key_components.description: Enhance capacity of the Water and Sanitation Sector Regulator (ERSAPS) to implement regulatory oversight, assist UWPs in selecting management models, establish volumetric tariff structures, negotiate and monitor Improvement Agreements, and promote transparency and accountability.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management, Communication, Outreach, Monitoring and Evaluation
key_components.description: Support for Project Management Unit (PMU) operations including fiduciary management, environmental and social standards implementation, monitoring and evaluation, gender strategies, citizen engagement, grievance redress mechanisms, and communication campaigns.
Provides mechanism for immediate response to eligible emergencies through reallocation of funds from other components if an eligible crisis or emergency is declared.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Honduras Strategic Investment Office (INVEST-H) under the General Coordinator of the Presidency of the Republic of Honduras
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Management Unit (PMU) within INVEST-H will coordinate with municipalities, UWPs, ERSAPS, COMAS (Municipal Councils for Water and Sanitation), USCLs (Local Supervision and Control Units), Honduras Water Utilities Association, and Honduras Municipalities Association. Tripartite Partnership Agreements between INVEST-H, municipalities, and UWPs. Inter-institutional Agreements for COVID-19 emergency response.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018). Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared. Procurement methods include: Request for Bids (goods and works), Quality-based selection (consulting services). Prior review thresholds apply. STEP system used for procurement planning and tracking.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: INVEST-H responsible for overall M&E in coordination with ERSAPS. Management Information System (MIS) with financial and results monitoring modules. Semi-annual and annual progress reports. Baseline reports, mid-term review, and final evaluation. ERSAPS monitors UWP performance and discloses information on public webpage. Independent annual audits by certified external auditors in compliance with International Standards on Auditing (ISA) and International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS).

identified_risks.risk_description: Political polarization and changes at central and municipal levels could delay approvals and divert focus from project objectives. Risk of declining municipal political commitment to politically sensitive reforms (micro-metering, volumetric tariffs) during electoral cycles. Potential diversion of counterpart funds to other priorities.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Municipal Corporation resolutions certifying commitment to volumetric tariffs and counterpart funding required prior to participation. Volumetric tariff structure requires endorsement from ERSAPS, Municipal Corporation, users, and local organizations (COMAS, USCL). ERSAPS' regulatory mandate helps offset political costs. Inter-institutional Partnership Agreements lay out commitments. INVEST-H maintains continuous dialogue with incoming administrations to maintain commitment.

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 pandemic and global downturn could weaken economic growth, external and fiscal accounts. Deeper economic decline could result from magnitude of shocks and slowdown in key trade partners. Remittances could decline significantly. Political polarization may delay water sector reforms crucial to economic sustainability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Government aims to strengthen fiscal sustainability through disease containment measures, Economic Rescue Plan financed by foreign exchange reserves (20% of GDP), Tax Code reform to increase revenue mobilization, and IMF precautionary program. Project includes emergency response component to address COVID-19 impacts on utilities.

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Project's innovative demand-based approach hinges on sufficient demand from service providers and could challenge existing capacities of INVEST-H, municipalities, and ERSAPS. INVEST-H's first time implementing demand-based operation. ERSAPS' technical capacity to undertake additional regulatory and oversight functions could be stretched.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project includes promotion of benefits to municipalities/UWPs and assistance in preparing applications. Substantial TA provided to INVEST-H for demand-based approach activities and to ERSAPS for monitoring and regulatory oversight. Stringent eligibility criteria tied to project funding. Minimum population size requirement (5,000 inhabitants) to promote operational and financial sustainability.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: INVEST-H's rapidly growing portfolio coupled with COVID-19 demands could challenge implementation capacities and cause delays. Project requires effective inter-institutional coordination between INVEST-H and range of municipalities and UWPs with different capacity levels. COVID pandemic could negatively impact utility revenues necessary for operations.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project will add qualified staff to PMU in INVEST-H. Subcomponent 1.5 can finance operational costs (chemicals, parts, maintenance) to alleviate pandemic's short-term impact on utility cashflows. Emphasis on institutional capacity building and technical assistance to participating UWPs.

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial management and procurement risks related to INVEST-H's capacity to manage complex inter-institutional arrangements and demand-based project implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Financial Management Assessment (FMA) concluded INVEST-H's capacity meets World Bank requirements. Action plan for strengthening FM arrangements includes IFR format/content, contract monitoring controls, POM incorporation of activities and flow of funds, and selection of FM staff and external auditors. Procurement capacity assessment conducted. PPSD prepared. Clear definition of roles and responsibilities in POM and Partnership Agreements. Fiduciary training for contractors.

identified_risks.risk_description: Negative community reaction to volumetric tariffs which could be cost-restrictive and increase tensions over water access. High levels of crime and violence in urban settings. Weak citizen engagement in water service management. Potential exclusion of Indigenous Peoples, Afro-descendants, and vulnerable groups from project benefits. Minor civil works could cause traffic disruptions, noise, dust, and construction waste.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive social outreach program during design and implementation. TA to UWPs on avoiding negative impacts on poor and vulnerable populations. Strengthening of local water governance structures (COMAS, USCLs). Social screening of sub-projects for Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants presence. Technical support to raise awareness and identify vulnerable groups. Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), Resettlement Framework (RF), Indigenous Peoples Planning Framework (IPPF), Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), and Labor Management Procedures (LMP) prepared and disclosed. Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) agreed. Environmental and Social Impact Assessments (ESIAs) and management plans to be prepared for each sub-project. Contractors required to maintain ESHS officers and implement mitigation measures.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of exclusion of vulnerable groups including Indigenous Peoples, Afro-descendants, people with disabilities, female-headed households, and illiterate individuals from project benefits and decision-making processes.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder Engagement Plan identifies affected and interested parties and vulnerable groups. Project-level grievance redress mechanism established. Citizen engagement events through existing platforms (COMAS, USCLs). Advocacy for including women's organizations and vulnerable groups in UWP boards and local governance structures. Beneficiary assessment surveys to promote accountability. Screening for vulnerable groups in each sub-project's environmental and social assessment. Capacity building for local authorities on needs of persons with disabilities and other vulnerable populations.

identified_risks.risk_description: Significant gender gaps in labor force participation (18% female vs 82% male in Honduras utilities vs 32% female in LAC region) and decision-making. Women underrepresented in technical, engineering, and management positions (3% in leadership vs 23% globally). Barriers in recruitment, retention, and promotion. Low female graduation rates in STEM fields.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: TA under Component 3 to develop gender policies and strategies for UWPs with >20,000 connections. Gender-sensitive recruitment practices, work-life balance policies, facilities, anti-sexual harassment policies, wage equity, training access, and mentorship programs. Targets for 10% female representation in leadership and technical positions. Advocacy for STEM education outreach and internships. Gender-based violence (GBV) screening conducted (determined low risk). GBV awareness raising and capacity building for UWP staff, municipalities, and contractors. Code of conduct in civil work contracts with GBV provisions.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7)
Projects on International Waterways (OP 7.50) - exception to notification requirement approved
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanism (GRM) established and managed by INVEST-H. User attention and information windows to be established in participating UWPs with adequate facilities, digital grievance registries, evidence of resolution within agreed timeframes, and service schedule information. Coordination mechanism for gathering claims and requests from UWPs and municipalities. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for project-affected communities. World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding Bank non-compliance with policies.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender gaps identified and addressed through: (1) Gender policies and strategies for UWPs with >20,000 connections; (2) Gender-sensitive recruitment, retention, and promotion practices; (3) Work-life balance and family-friendly policies; (4) Anti-sexual harassment policies and training; (5) Wage equity measures; (6) Training and mentorship programs for women; (7) Targets for 10% female representation in leadership and technical positions; (8) Advocacy for STEM education and internships; (9) Gender-based violence (GBV) awareness and capacity building; (10) Code of conduct in contracts with GBV provisions; (11) Monitoring through Results Framework indicators.
vulnerable_groups: Healthcare centers and schools (as priority beneficiaries)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Honduras faces high levels of crime and violence, particularly in urban settings, which is identified as a key social risk. The project operates in this challenging security context. Mitigation measures include: (1) Comprehensive social outreach and communication campaigns to build community buy-in; (2) Strengthening of local governance structures (COMAS, USCLs) to promote citizen engagement and voice; (3) Grievance redress mechanisms to address community concerns; (4) Technical assistance to UWPs on avoiding negative impacts on vulnerable populations; (5) Inclusive decision-making processes involving civil society; (6) Focus on service improvements to build trust and reduce tensions over water access. The project's emphasis on volumetric tariffs and cost recovery could create tensions if not managed carefully through transparent communication and support for vulnerable groups.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Honduras is not classified as a fragile state, but faces significant fragility factors: (1) High poverty rates (48% below poverty line, 23% extreme poverty); (2) High inequality (Gini coefficient 52.1); (3) High exposure to climate change and natural disasters (loses 2.6% of GDP annually to climatic events); (4) High levels of urban crime and violence; (5) Weak institutions and governance; (6) Limited economic diversification and productivity growth; (7) Political instability and polarization; (8) Weak municipal capacity; (9) Decentralization challenges; (10) COVID-19 pandemic impacts on economy and health. These factors create implementation risks and sustainability challenges that the project addresses through institutional strengthening, capacity building, and participatory approaches.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High levels of crime and violence in urban areas where project operates. This affects: (1) Implementation security for project staff and contractors; (2) Community safety and willingness to participate; (3) Utility staff safety; (4) Asset security for water infrastructure. Mitigation includes: (1) Community engagement to build local support; (2) Strengthening local governance and citizen oversight; (3) Grievance mechanisms to address community concerns; (4) Focus on service improvements to build trust; (5) Codes of conduct for contractors addressing security and safety issues.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No - The project does not involve land acquisition or involuntary resettlement. However, a Resettlement Framework (RF) was prepared as a precautionary measure given the demand-based approach and potential for minor civil works. The RF provides procedures for addressing any unforeseen land-related issues. The project focuses on rehabilitation and upgrading of existing water systems within their original footprints, which minimizes displacement risks. Environmental and Social Impact Assessments (ESIAs) for each sub-project will screen for any potential land-related impacts.
